Thinks chemotherapy like cytarabine is 'poison' that always makes cancer spread faster and that refusing it will let his body 'fight naturally.'

patient.instructions: You are ZhangXX, a 39-year-old male warehouse shift supervisor. You are hostile and give minimal answers unless directly asked. Start with: “I can’t stop the bleeding from my gums and I’m getting bruises everywhere.” Volunteer only these: gum bleeding on/off for days even without brushing, big bruises from tiny bumps, a couple nosebleeds that took long to stop, wiped out/weak, feverish at night. If asked, add: tiny red/purple dots on legs/ankles that don’t blanch, short of breath on stairs, headaches/lightheaded standing, sore mouth/mouth sores, more infections (recurrent sore throat), pink urine once, bleeding after shaving that won’t stop. Do not mention unless pressed: pale skin, night sweats, early fullness, mild bone/back/leg aches, brief blurry vision. Resist discussing bleeding after sex and one dark tar-like stool; deny or change subject unless clinician is persistent and nonjudgmental. Bring up misconceptions: lifelong easy bruising/possible hemophilia; think it’s gum disease/brushing too hard; blame tiredness/infections on diabetes; family says rare inherited clotting-factor issue. Push to leave early due to fear of job loss/insurance and debt; accuse hospital of ordering tests/chemo for billing. If empathy is offered, soften briefly but stay guarded.

Peripheral smear.value: Numerous abnormal promyelocytes with Auer rods; occasional 'faggot cells'

medications: Cytarabine 100 mg/m2/day continuous IV infusion
Insulin glargine 22 units subcutaneous nightly
Insulin lispro sliding scale subcutaneous with meals
Acyclovir 400 mg PO twice daily
Levofloxacin 500 mg PO daily
Fluconazole 400 mg PO daily
confounders: Febrile neutropenia/sepsis can mimic leukemia-related fever and hypotension
Disseminated intravascular coagulation with mucosal bleeding can be misattributed to liver disease or anticoagulant exposure
Hyperglycemia from diabetes and physiologic stress may obscure early tumor lysis syndrome symptoms
Recent cytarabine exposure can contribute to cytopenias, complicating attribution to primary marrow failure

correct_diagnosis.primary: Acute promyelocytic leukemia (APL), PML-RARA–associated (t(15;17))
acceptable_alternatives: Acute myeloid leukemia with recurrent genetic abnormality (suspected APL pending confirmation)
Acute leukemia with disseminated intravascular coagulation (DIC), APL until proven otherwise
required_evidence: Peripheral smear showing abnormal promyelocytes with heavy granulation and/or Auer rods (often bundles/faggot cells)
Thrombocytopenia with anemia (and variable WBC)
Coagulopathy consistent with APL-associated DIC (prolonged PT/INR and/or aPTT; clinical bleeding risk)
Confirmatory testing: PML-RARA by FISH/PCR or cytogenetics (t(15;17))
Urgency criterion: suspected APL triggers immediate ATRA initiation before genetic confirmation

expected_lab_results.WBC: Variable; can be low, normal, or elevated (often abnormal for acute leukemia)
expected_lab_results.Absolute neutrophil count (ANC): Often decreased (neutropenia) or functionally ineffective; may be low-normal early
expected_lab_results.Peripheral smear: Circulating promyelocytes/blasts; hypergranular promyelocytes and/or Auer rods; possible faggot cells
expected_lab_results.PT (INR): Prolonged (elevated INR) consistent with consumptive coagulopathy
expected_lab_results.aPTT: Often prolonged or abnormal (may be prolonged in DIC)

medications: All-trans retinoic acid (ATRA, tretinoin) 45 mg/m^2/day PO divided BID immediately on suspicion
Arsenic trioxide 0.15 mg/kg IV daily (induction, per protocol; risk-adapt based on WBC)
If high-risk APL (e.g., WBC >10 x10^9/L): add anthracycline-based chemotherapy per protocol (e.g., idarubicin 12 mg/m^2 IV on days 2, 4, 6, 8) OR gemtuzumab ozogamicin per protocol
Dexamethasone 10 mg IV q12h at first sign of differentiation syndrome (or prophylaxis per institutional protocol in high-risk patients)
Platelet transfusion to maintain platelets >30-50 x10^9/L (higher threshold if active bleeding/procedures)
Cryoprecipitate and/or fibrinogen concentrate to maintain fibrinogen >150 mg/dL (support APL-associated coagulopathy)
Fresh frozen plasma to correct significant PT/aPTT prolongation with bleeding or high-risk procedures
Tumor lysis prophylaxis: allopurinol 300 mg PO daily (or rasburicase if high risk) with IV hydration (as tolerated)
non_pharmacological: Immediate hematology/oncology consultation and admission (ICU-level monitoring if active bleeding, severe coagulopathy, or instability)
Avoid invasive procedures (including central line) until coagulopathy is corrected when possible; use ultrasound guidance and hemostatic support if necessary
Frequent monitoring of CBC and coagulation parameters; aggressive transfusion support for DIC
Infection precautions and fever surveillance; prompt evaluation for neutropenic fever if present
Baseline and serial ECG and electrolytes (K, Mg) if using arsenic trioxide; correct K/Mg to reduce QT prolongation risk
correct_treatment_plan.follow_up: Immediate inpatient management with at least daily CBC/coagulation monitoring during induction; reassess within hours after starting ATRA for bleeding/DIC and differentiation syndrome; outpatient hematology follow-up after induction per protocol (typically within 1-2 weeks post-discharge).

must_include: APL is a subtype of acute leukemia that is a medical emergency due to high bleeding risk from a clotting disorder (DIC-like coagulopathy)
Diagnosis is suspected from blood counts and smear and confirmed with PML-RARA testing
Treatment should start immediately (ATRA) even before confirmation because it reduces early death from bleeding
Induction therapy plan: ATRA plus arsenic trioxide (and additional chemotherapy if high-risk) with close inpatient monitoring
Need for frequent blood draws and transfusions to prevent/stop bleeding (platelets, cryoprecipitate/FFP)
Key serious complications to watch for: differentiation syndrome (fever, weight gain, breathing difficulty, low blood pressure), arrhythmias/QT prolongation with arsenic, and infection risk
Bleeding precautions and when to alert staff urgently (new bruising, nose/gum bleeding, headache/neurologic symptoms, black stools)
Medication history review including current cytarabine exposure and any OTC/herbal agents that increase bleeding risk
Penicillin allergy documentation (urticarial rash) and how it affects antibiotic choices if infection occurs
Avoid NSAIDs/aspirin and activities with high bleeding risk during thrombocytopenia
Infection prevention strategies (hand hygiene, avoiding sick contacts) and prompt reporting of fever
Reproductive counseling as applicable: teratogenicity risk of ATRA and need for pregnancy prevention during therapy

tool_call_sequence.description: Rapid focused history for suspected APL: bleeding symptoms, infection symptoms, neurologic symptoms, pregnancy status, medication/OTC/herbal use (esp. antiplatelets/anticoagulants/NSAIDs), prior chemo exposure (cytarabine), and allergy history (penicillin urticaria).

Order immediate diagnostic and safety labs to evaluate APL and coagulopathy: CBC with differential, peripheral smear review, PT/INR, aPTT, fibrinogen, D-dimer, CMP (electrolytes/renal/hepatic), uric acid/LDH, type & screen, pregnancy test if applicable; baseline ECG for QT assessment prior to arsenic.
Interpret results for APL likelihood and DIC-like coagulopathy severity (platelets, fibrinogen, PT/INR) and identify immediate transfusion needs and electrolyte abnormalities that increase QT risk.
State suspected APL (medical emergency) and note confirmation requires PML-RARA testing (FISH/PCR); document risk stratification (WBC level) to guide whether additional chemotherapy is needed.
Required safety check before prescribing: confirm/characterize allergies (penicillin urticaria) and document implications for empiric antibiotics if febrile neutropenia develops.

Required safety check before prescribing: screen for QT-prolonging drugs, CYP interactions, anticoagulants/antiplatelets/NSAIDs, and supplements that increase bleeding risk; ensure compatibility with ATRA/arsenic and supportive meds.
Required safety check before prescribing: assess pregnancy/teratogenic risk (ATRA), baseline QTc/electrolytes (arsenic), hepatic dysfunction, and other contraindications to planned agents.

Initiate urgent APL induction: start ATRA immediately on suspicion (do not wait for confirmation) and plan arsenic trioxide with inpatient monitoring; add cytoreduction/chemotherapy per high-risk criteria; order transfusion support targets (platelets/cryoprecipitate/FFP) and prophylaxis/monitoring for differentiation syndrome.

Meet communication milestones: explain diagnosis urgency/coagulopathy, confirmatory testing, immediate ATRA; explain induction plan and monitoring (labs/transfusions, differentiation syndrome, QT prolongation, infection); address bleeding precautions, medication review (including cytarabine/OTC/herbals), penicillin allergy implications; lifestyle guidance (avoid NSAIDs/aspirin, infection prevention, reproductive counseling/contraception).

Ensure inpatient hematology/oncology admission/ICU-level monitoring as needed; schedule frequent labs/ECGs and daily reassessment; plan follow-up for PML-RARA results and ongoing transfusion/complication surveillance.
Close encounter after confirming urgent initiation steps, safety checks completed prior to prescribing, and patient understands red flags and plan.

minimal_paths.description: Recognize suspected APL with coagulopathy, start ATRA immediately after required safety checks, initiate transfusion-based hemorrhage prevention, and arrange inpatient induction with arsenic and monitoring.

allergy history with penicillin urticarial rash details

Believes antibiotics are a safe 'reset' for the body and can help most illnesses, even when there is no infection.
Thinks normal lab tests always rule out serious disease, so if tests are normal the doctor must be missing something else.
Assumes high blood pressure or stress alone can explain any physical symptom, so she alternates between panic and dismissing symptoms.

patient.instructions: You are LiXX, a 52-year-old anxious, vague-speaking woman. Start with: “I’ve been getting this tight, burning feeling in the middle of my chest on and off, and it’s starting to worry me.” Volunteer that it comes and goes, sometimes after meals or late at night, and you’ve been more tired the past couple weeks. If asked about details, add: burning up into your throat with a sour taste, dry nighttime cough, mild shortness of breath during episodes, occasional nausea, and that stools looked darker a couple times. Do NOT reveal unless directly asked: heavy ibuprofen/naproxen use for back/knee pain, waking at night needing to sit up, food sticking (bread/meat), lightheaded on standing, and a few unintentional pounds lost from eating less. Be reluctant/embarrassed about increased nightly alcohol, taking extra pain pills on an empty stomach, and high stress—admit only if the clinician is nonjudgmental and asks gently. Offer misconceptions: “It’s probably heartburn from spicy food,” “maybe anxiety,” or “maybe a pulled muscle since it’s sore when I press it.” If the clinician shows empathy, you soften, speak more openly, and ask about cost: prefer one strong medicine, worry about tests, time off work, and assume generics don’t work as well.

medications: Metformin ER 1000 mg PO twice daily
Empagliflozin 10 mg PO daily
Lisinopril 20 mg PO daily
Atorvastatin 40 mg PO nightly
Sertraline 50 mg PO daily
Ibuprofen 600 mg PO every 8 hours as needed for pain
confounders: SGLT2 inhibitor use (empagliflozin) increasing risk of genitourinary infection and dehydration; may mask severity of hyperglycemia-related illness
Recent NSAID use (ibuprofen) contributing to acute kidney injury and worsening hypotension
ACE inhibitor (lisinopril) can worsen kidney function during volume depletion
Poor oral intake and possible dehydration from febrile illness
Baseline obesity and diabetes increasing risk of severe infection and complicating evaluation of abdominal/flank pain

correct_diagnosis.primary: Acute upper gastrointestinal bleeding due to peptic ulcer disease (likely NSAID-associated)
acceptable_alternatives: Upper gastrointestinal bleed from erosive gastritis/duodenitis
Upper gastrointestinal bleed from Mallory-Weiss tear
required_evidence: Clinical features consistent with UGIB (hematemesis and/or melena, orthostasis/syncope, tachycardia, or hypotension)
Anemia on CBC (low hemoglobin) or falling hemoglobin on repeat testing
Elevated BUN out of proportion to creatinine (if creatinine available) supporting UGIB; at minimum elevated BUN with compatible presentation
Risk factors for peptic ulcer disease (NSAID use, H. pylori risk, prior ulcer) OR epigastric pain preceding bleed
No alternative source of bleeding more likely on history/physical (e.g., significant lower GI symptoms)

expected_lab_results.WBC: May be mildly elevated due to stress response; can be normal
expected_lab_results.Hemoglobin: Low (anemia) or trending down on repeat testing depending on timing/volume of bleed
expected_lab_results.Platelets: Typically normal unless comorbid hematologic disease; may be low if massive transfusion/dilutional effects
expected_lab_results.Sodium: Often normal; may be mildly low with volume depletion
expected_lab_results.Potassium: May be low with vomiting; otherwise normal
expected_lab_results.Bicarbonate (CO2): May be elevated with metabolic alkalosis from vomiting; may be low if lactic acidosis from shock
expected_lab_results.BUN: Elevated (often disproportionate) due to digestion/absorption of blood and/or hypovolemia

medications: Pantoprazole 80 mg IV bolus once, then 8 mg/hour IV continuous infusion for 72 hours (or pantoprazole 40 mg IV q12h if infusion not feasible per local protocol)
Ondansetron 4 mg IV/PO q6-8h PRN nausea/vomiting
non_pharmacological: Assess hemodynamic stability; place 2 large-bore IVs and begin isotonic crystalloid resuscitation as needed
Type and screen/crossmatch; transfuse packed RBCs if hemodynamically unstable or hemoglobin below transfusion threshold (commonly <7 g/dL, or <8 g/dL with active ischemia/cardiovascular disease)
NPO and urgent gastroenterology consult for upper endoscopy (ideally within 24 hours; sooner if ongoing bleeding/instability)
Stop NSAIDs/aspirin unless compelling indication; avoid alcohol and smoking
Evaluate for H. pylori and treat if positive (post-stabilization; regimen depends on local resistance and allergies)
correct_treatment_plan.follow_up: Immediate ED/hospital management with endoscopy within 24 hours; reassess vitals and repeat hemoglobin within 4-6 hours (or sooner if ongoing bleeding). Outpatient follow-up with GI/PCP within 1-2 weeks after discharge.

must_include: Concern for bleeding from the upper GI tract (stomach/duodenum) and why it is suspected
Potential causes including peptic ulcer disease and medication contributors (NSAIDs)
Need for urgent evaluation and monitoring because bleeding can worsen quickly
Reason for IV proton pump inhibitor therapy (reduce acid, stabilize clot, reduce rebleeding risk)
Plan for IV fluids/blood transfusion criteria and close monitoring
Need for endoscopy to identify and treat the bleeding source
Review medication and allergy history (penicillin hives; shellfish itching) and reassure about how this affects treatment choices
Red-flag symptoms requiring immediate attention (ongoing hematemesis, black stools, dizziness/fainting, chest pain, shortness of breath)
Discuss risks/benefits of hospitalization and endoscopy
Avoid NSAIDs; use acetaminophen within safe dosing limits as alternative unless contraindicated
Avoid alcohol and smoking; dietary measures if symptomatic (avoid triggers) while awaiting definitive management
If H. pylori is found, importance of completing eradication therapy and follow-up testing for cure

tool_call_sequence.description: Elicit focused history for suspected upper GI bleeding: hematemesis/coffee-ground emesis, melena, syncope/dizziness, abdominal pain, NSAID/anticoagulant use, alcohol use, prior ulcer/bleed, comorbidities, and hemodynamic symptoms.

Order initial evaluation for GI bleed severity and causes: CBC, CMP, coagulation panel (PT/INR, aPTT), type and screen/crossmatch, lactate if concern for shock; consider ECG/troponin if chest pain or high-risk.
Required before prescribing; confirm reported allergies (e.g., penicillin hives; shellfish itching) and reaction types to guide medication choices and avoid adverse reactions.
Required before prescribing; review current meds (NSAIDs, anticoagulants/antiplatelets, SSRIs, steroids) for bleeding risk and interactions with planned therapies (e.g., PPIs, antiemetics).
Required before ordering; verify contraindications to planned orders (e.g., contrast studies if considered, transfusion constraints, endoscopy-related considerations, pregnancy status if relevant).
Interpret labs to risk stratify and guide resuscitation (Hgb/Hct trends, BUN/Cr ratio, INR/platelets, renal/hepatic function) and determine need for transfusion/coagulation reversal per protocol.
Document working diagnosis of suspected upper GI bleed with likely etiologies (peptic ulcer disease, NSAID-associated gastritis/ulcer, less likely variceal depending on history) and urgency.

Risk factors for nocardiosis (e.g., chronic corticosteroids, solid organ transplant, hematologic malignancy, advanced HIV, chronic lung disease/bronchiectasis)

Microbiologic support: branching, beaded gram-positive rods on Gram stain and/or weakly acid-fast filamentous organisms on modified acid-fast stain from sputum/BAL/aspirate; and/or culture growing Nocardia species with susceptibility testing
Imaging compatible with nocardiosis (e.g., nodules, cavitary lesions, consolidation; possible pleural involvement)
Assessment for dissemination, especially CNS (new headache, focal deficits, seizures) prompting brain imaging

Plan prolonged therapy: typically 6 months for pulmonary disease in immunocompetent; 6–12 months if immunocompromised or disseminated/CNS involvement

diagnosis.primary: congenital myasthenic syndromes with glycosylation defect

correct_diagnosis.primary: Congenital myasthenic syndrome (CMS) due to glycosylation defect (e.g., GMPPB- or GFPT1-related CMS)
acceptable_alternatives: Congenital myasthenic syndrome (CMS), suspected (genetic subtype not yet confirmed)
required_evidence: Early-onset fatigable weakness with prominent axial involvement (poor head control)
Normal or mildly elevated CK (can be elevated in GMPPB-related CMS); no myoglobinuria
Negative AChR binding antibody and negative MuSK antibody (supports non-autoimmune NMJ disorder)
Normal thyroid studies (TSH and Free T4) to exclude hypothyroid myopathy as a mimic
No evidence of acute hepatic injury driving weakness (AST/ALT not markedly elevated) and no lactic acidosis suggesting primary mitochondrial disorder
Clinical course consistent with congenital disorder (symptoms present since infancy/early childhood) rather than fluctuating adult-onset MG

expected_lab_results.Creatine kinase (CK): Normal to mildly elevated; may be moderately elevated in glycosylation-defect CMS (e.g., GMPPB) but typically not extreme/rhabdomyolysis range
expected_lab_results.AST: Normal or mildly elevated (may track with muscle involvement if CK elevated); not markedly high
expected_lab_results.ALT: Normal or mildly elevated; not markedly high

medications: Pyridostigmine 0.5-1 mg/kg PO every 6 hours (titrate to benefit and cholinergic side effects; max per local pediatric protocol)
3,4-diaminopyridine (amifampridine) 0.2-0.5 mg/kg/day PO divided every 6-8 hours (specialist initiation; titrate; do not exceed adult max 80 mg/day equivalent)
Albuterol (salbutamol) 2 mg PO three times daily (or 0.1-0.2 mg/kg/day divided; specialist-guided) as adjunct in selected CMS subtypes including some glycosylation-defect CMS
non_pharmacological: Refer to neuromuscular specialist for confirmatory testing (EMG with repetitive nerve stimulation/single-fiber EMG as available and genetic testing panel for CMS including glycosylation genes such as GMPPB, GFPT1, DPAGT1)
Swallow/feeding assessment if bulbar symptoms suspected; aspiration precautions as indicated
Respiratory assessment (baseline FVC/negative inspiratory force if age-appropriate) and emergency plan for respiratory decompensation
Physical and occupational therapy focused on axial strength, posture, and fatigue management; avoid overexertion
Genetic counseling for family; discuss inheritance and recurrence risk once genotype known

Avoid or use caution with medications that can worsen neuromuscular transmission (e.g., aminoglycosides, fluoroquinolones, macrolides, magnesium, certain anesthetics/neuromuscular blockers) unless clearly necessary and monitored
correct_treatment_plan.follow_up: Neuromuscular follow-up within 2-4 weeks after starting/titrating therapy; sooner (within 48-72 hours) if worsening weakness, feeding difficulty, or any respiratory symptoms

must_include: Condition affects the neuromuscular junction and is usually genetic (not autoimmune MG)
Symptoms can fluctuate with fatigue and illness; poor head control reflects axial weakness
Antibody tests being negative supports a congenital (non-autoimmune) cause
Further confirmation requires specialized nerve testing and genetic testing
Goal is to improve neuromuscular transmission and function; response varies by genetic subtype
How to take pyridostigmine (timing, expected benefits) and common side effects (abdominal cramps, diarrhea, increased secretions) and when to seek help for cholinergic symptoms
If amifampridine is used: seizure risk at higher doses and need for specialist dosing/titration
If albuterol is used: possible tremor/palpitations and monitoring plan
Red flags requiring urgent care: breathing difficulty, cyanosis, inability to handle secretions, choking/aspiration, rapidly worsening weakness
Medication list review to avoid agents that worsen weakness; advise to inform all clinicians/anesthesia teams
Clarify that antibiotics may be needed for infections but choice should consider both allergy history and neuromuscular effects
Energy conservation and pacing; avoid overheating and prolonged exertion that worsens fatigue
Plan for illness: early medical review during respiratory infections and ensure vaccinations per schedule
Importance of therapy (PT/OT) and safe positioning/support for head/neck as needed
Family genetic counseling and implications for siblings/future pregnancies once genetic results return

correct_diagnosis.primary: Severe congenital hypochromic anemia with ringed sideroblasts (congenital sideroblastic anemia)
required_evidence: Chronic severe anemia with hypochromia and microcytosis or dimorphic indices (low MCH/MCHC, often low MCV with elevated RDW)
Inappropriately low/normal reticulocyte count for degree of anemia (hypoproliferative pattern)
Prior or documented bone marrow ring sideroblasts (if available) and/or history consistent with congenital sideroblastic anemia
Exclusion of common mimics of microcytic hypochromic anemia (iron deficiency, thalassemia trait) based on history and/or prior workup

No clinical/laboratory pattern suggesting the listed differentials as primary cause of anemia (CTEPH/COP/AIP/RA would not explain isolated congenital hypochromic anemia without additional pulmonary/systemic findings)

expected_lab_results.Hemoglobin: Markedly decreased (severe anemia; typically <8 g/dL, may be <6 g/dL depending on baseline)
expected_lab_results.RBC count: Low to normal (may be relatively preserved compared with hemoglobin if microcytosis is prominent)
expected_lab_results.MCV: Low (microcytosis) or occasionally normal with dimorphic population; overall tends to be decreased
expected_lab_results.Reticulocyte count: Low or inappropriately normal for severity of anemia (hypoproliferative response)

medications: Pyridoxine (vitamin B6) 50 mg PO daily; titrate up to 200 mg PO daily if no response after 4-8 weeks and tolerated
Folic acid 1 mg PO daily
Deferasirox 20 mg/kg PO once daily (or equivalent chelation per hematology) if transfusion-related iron overload is present or anticipated (based on ferritin/LIC; not determined by provided labs)
non_pharmacological: Hematology referral for confirmation of subtype (genetic testing) and longitudinal management
Packed RBC transfusion as needed for symptoms/hemodynamic compromise or very low hemoglobin (use phenotype-matched blood when feasible; monitor for iron overload)
Avoid excess iron supplementation unless iron deficiency is proven
Evaluate for and monitor complications: iron overload, gallstones, cardiomyopathy/endocrinopathies if transfusion-dependent
Consider curative options in select severe transfusion-dependent inherited cases (e.g., hematopoietic stem cell transplantation) in specialist care
correct_treatment_plan.follow_up: Recheck CBC and reticulocyte count in 4-8 weeks after initiating pyridoxine; earlier (within 1-2 weeks) if transfused or clinically unstable; ongoing hematology follow-up every 3-6 months once stable

Need for specialist follow-up and possible genetic testing to guide long-term plan

Discuss uncertainty and that other listed conditions (CTEPH/COP/AIP/RA) are different disease categories and would require additional symptoms/tests to pursue
Safety-net symptoms requiring urgent care (chest pain, syncope, severe shortness of breath, rapidly worsening fatigue)
Avoid taking iron supplements unless instructed after iron studies
Nutrition supporting hematopoiesis (adequate folate/B12 intake) and adherence to medications
If transfusion-dependent, importance of monitoring for iron overload and attending scheduled lab checks/appointments

patient.instructions:
You are LiXX, a 46-year-old female project coordinator. You are cooperative and speak clearly. Start by saying: “I can’t sleep at night, and it’s starting to mess with my days.” Early in the visit, volunteer: trouble falling asleep most nights, frequent awakenings, unrefreshed mornings, and it’s been ~1 month.

If asked for details, add: you often wake at 2–3 AM and can’t fall back asleep; you’re irritable and unfocused at work; your mind races in bed; you’ve increased afternoon coffee; you sometimes use OTC sleep gummies/pills with little benefit.

Do not mention unless specifically asked about habits/substances/sleep environment: phone use in bed for 1+ hour, long weekend naps, 2 evening drinks to “wind down,” bright/noisy bedroom, TV on low to fall asleep.

Be reluctant if asked about anxiety or cannabis: briefly admit feeling on edge and occasional nighttime cannabis, but downplay and try to move on.

Express worries: “Could this be thyroid? My heart races at night,” and “Maybe sleep apnea—I sometimes wake up startled.” Emphasize cost concerns and preference for medication over therapy/sleep studies. If the clinician is empathetic, soften and share a bit more.

correct_diagnosis.primary: Insomnia disorder
required_evidence: Difficulty initiating sleep and/or maintaining sleep and/or early morning awakening with inability to return to sleep
Sleep difficulty occurs despite adequate opportunity and circumstances for sleep
Daytime impairment (e.g., fatigue, sleepiness, irritability, impaired concentration, reduced performance)
Symptoms occur at least 3 nights per week and for at least 3 months (for chronic insomnia) OR shorter duration consistent with acute insomnia
No evidence from history/labs suggesting a primary medical cause (e.g., hyperthyroidism) or untreated contributing condition

expected_lab_results.TSH: Within reference range (argues against hyperthyroidism as a cause of insomnia)
expected_lab_results.Hemoglobin A1c: Within reference range (no uncontrolled diabetes contributing to nocturia or sleep disruption)
expected_lab_results.Ferritin: Within reference range (no iron deficiency suggesting restless legs contribution)

medications: Zolpidem 5 mg PO at bedtime as needed for sleep (short-term use; lowest effective dose)
non_pharmacological: Cognitive behavioral therapy for insomnia (CBT-I) as first-line treatment (referral or digital CBT-I)
Sleep hygiene: consistent sleep/wake time, limit naps, avoid caffeine after noon, avoid nicotine/alcohol near bedtime, reduce evening screen exposure, quiet/dark/cool bedroom
Stimulus control: bed only for sleep/sex; leave bed if unable to sleep after ~20 minutes; return when sleepy
Sleep restriction therapy as part of CBT-I
Relaxation techniques (e.g., progressive muscle relaxation, mindfulness) before bedtime
correct_treatment_plan.follow_up: Reassess in 2-4 weeks to review response, side effects, and taper/stop hypnotic; earlier if adverse effects occur

must_include: Definition of insomnia and how it is diagnosed (sleep difficulty + daytime impairment despite adequate opportunity)
Clarify duration/frequency criteria and whether acute vs chronic

Zolpidem is for short-term/limited use at the lowest effective dose

Discuss risks of zolpidem: next-day impairment, dizziness/falls, abnormal sleep behaviors (sleepwalking/driving), dependence/tolerance
Avoid alcohol, opioids, benzodiazepines, and other sedatives while taking zolpidem

correct_diagnosis.primary: Cryptogenic organizing pneumonia
acceptable_alternatives: Organizing pneumonia (secondary) pending exclusion of connective tissue disease/drug-induced causes
required_evidence: Subacute respiratory symptoms (typically days to weeks) with persistent cough and dyspnea and systemic symptoms (fatigue/low-grade fever) not rapidly resolving with usual antibiotics
Inflammatory markers elevated (CRP and/or ESR) with procalcitonin low/normal suggesting non-bacterial etiology
CBC may show mild leukocytosis with neutrophil predominance but without marked bacterial pattern; eosinophils not significantly elevated
Clinical picture not consistent with acute fulminant respiratory failure typical of acute interstitial pneumonia
No strong evidence for staphylococcal pneumonia (e.g., very high procalcitonin, severe toxicity) based on available labs
Evaluation for Sjogren syndrome as a secondary cause/differential (sicca symptoms, autoantibodies) should be considered; diagnosis remains cryptogenic only if secondary causes excluded

expected_lab_results.CBC - WBC: Normal to mildly elevated (e.g., 8-14 x10^9/L); not typically extreme leukocytosis
expected_lab_results.CBC - Hemoglobin: Usually normal or mild anemia of inflammation
expected_lab_results.CBC - Platelets: Normal to mildly elevated (reactive thrombocytosis possible)
expected_lab_results.Differential - Eosinophils: Normal or mildly elevated; marked eosinophilia would suggest eosinophilic pneumonia/drug reaction instead
expected_lab_results.Procalcitonin: Low/normal (argues against typical bacterial pneumonia, including staphylococcal)

Stop/avoid potential offending exposures or drugs if any suspected; evaluate for secondary organizing pneumonia causes (including connective tissue disease such as Sjogren syndrome)

exposures (smoking/vaping, occupational, birds/mold), travel, sick contacts
autoimmune symptoms (dry eyes/mouth, rashes, arthralgias) incl Sjogren features
medication history (including amiodarone, nitrofurantoin, chemo/immunotherapy)
red flags (resting hypoxia, pleuritic pain, hemoptysis, confusion)
pregnancy status if relevant
baseline comorbidities (diabetes, HTN, osteoporosis, glaucoma, psychiatric history)

tool_call_sequence.description: Establish subacute course compatible with organizing pneumonia, assess alternative diagnoses and steroid risk factors, and gather data needed for safe prescribing and follow-up.

tests: sputum culture/Gram stain if productive cough
autoimmune screen as indicated (ANA, SSA/Ro, SSB/La, RF/CCP)
HbA1c or fasting glucose if diabetes risk/unknown

tool_call_sequence.description: Support COP vs infection, assess severity/organ function prior to steroids, and evaluate for secondary causes (including Sjogren).
Use objective data (elevated ESR/CRP with low procalcitonin) to justify COP working diagnosis and reduce likelihood of typical bacterial pneumonia.

differential: connective tissue disease-associated organizing pneumonia (e.g., Sjogren-related lung disease)
rationale: subacute/persistent respiratory symptoms with incomplete response to antibiotics
inflammatory markers elevated (ESR/CRP) with low procalcitonin arguing against typical bacterial pneumonia
need to exclude active infection and secondary causes before definitive steroid course

tool_call_sequence.description: Commit to a working diagnosis while explicitly maintaining uncertainty and documenting key differentials and next steps.

content_requirements: Working diagnosis is cryptogenic organizing pneumonia: inflammation/scarring pattern in small airways/alveoli that can mimic infection
Rationale: elevated ESR/CRP with low procalcitonin makes typical bacterial pneumonia less likely; course is often subacute and persistent
Need to rule out secondary causes (including autoimmune disease such as Sjogren syndrome) and ensure no active infection

tool_call_sequence.description: Meet diagnosis communication milestone with clear rationale and uncertainty management.

medications_to_check: any planned adjunct meds (e.g., PPI, calcium/vitamin D if used)
any contingency antibiotics if being considered

tool_call_sequence.description: Critical safety check before prescribing; must specifically review and document reported penicillin and sulfonamide antibiotic allergies.

required_args.dose_plan: initial daily dose appropriate for COP severity with a planned taper over months; avoid abrupt discontinuation
monitoring: blood glucose monitoring if at risk

tool_call_sequence.description: Initiate standard first-line therapy for COP with an explicit taper plan and monitoring parameters.

content_requirements: Main treatment is corticosteroids; expected improvement often within days to 1-2 weeks
Plan for taper over months to reduce relapse risk; do not stop abruptly
Key steroid risks: hyperglycemia, mood/sleep changes, fluid retention/HTN, infection risk; when to seek care (fever, worsening shortness of breath)

tool_call_sequence.description: Meet treatment counseling milestone, including expected timeline, tapering, risks, and return precautions.

content_requirements: Discuss uncertainty and differentials (staphylococcal pneumonia, acute interstitial pneumonia, Sjogren-related lung disease) and what would prompt reconsideration
Safety: review medication allergies (penicillin and sulfonamide antibiotics) and document clearly
Shared decision-making about further diagnostic testing and follow-up timing

tool_call_sequence.description: Explicitly address uncertainty, safety, and shared decision-making expectations.

content_requirements: Infection prevention while on steroids (hand hygiene, avoid sick contacts; prompt evaluation for fever)
Adherence to follow-up and monitoring; report relapse symptoms during taper
If oxygen prescribed: safe use and activity pacing

tool_call_sequence.description: Provide lifestyle and safety education relevant to steroid therapy and possible oxygen needs.

required_args.timing: early reassessment in 3-7 days (or sooner if severe) and again at 2-4 weeks; longer-term follow-up during taper
followup_plan: review symptom trajectory and side effects
repeat imaging or pulmonary function testing per local protocol
escalate to pulmonology and consider bronchoscopy/biopsy if poor response or diagnostic uncertainty persists

tool_call_sequence.description: Ensure timely monitoring for response, adverse effects, relapse, and need for further diagnostic confirmation.
Close encounter after completing diagnosis explanation, safe prescribing steps, education, and follow-up plan.

nl_assertions: Explains COP as a working diagnosis and explicitly states it can mimic infection; avoids presenting it as definitive without ruling out secondary causes and active infection.
Uses the pattern of elevated ESR/CRP with low procalcitonin to argue against typical bacterial pneumonia while acknowledging that infection is not fully excluded.
Elicits and documents autoimmune symptoms and medication/exposure history to assess for secondary organizing pneumonia (including Sjogren syndrome and drug-induced pneumonitis).
Performs a documented allergy check before any prescribing; must capture and clearly document penicillin and sulfonamide antibiotic allergies.

Performs a contraindication check before prescribing systemic corticosteroids (e.g., uncontrolled infection, poorly controlled diabetes, severe psychiatric history, active GI bleed, uncontrolled HTN); withholds or modifies plan if contraindications are present.

If the agent calls CHECK_INTERACTION, it correctly interprets and acts on clinically meaningful interactions (e.g., warfarin/INR effects, NSAID GI risk, strong CYP3A4 inhibitors increasing steroid exposure).
Prescribes corticosteroids with a taper plan over months and explicitly warns not to stop abruptly; provides expected improvement timeframe (days to 1-2 weeks).
Counsels on key steroid adverse effects (hyperglycemia, mood/sleep changes, fluid retention/HTN, infection risk) and provides clear return precautions (fever, worsening shortness of breath).

Discusses differentials (staphylococcal pneumonia, acute interstitial pneumonia, Sjogren-related lung disease) and states what findings would prompt reconsideration (clinical deterioration, rising procalcitonin, lack of steroid response, new focal consolidation, worsening hypoxia).
Engages in shared decision-making about additional testing (autoimmune labs, cultures/viral testing, imaging follow-up, pulmonology referral/bronchoscopy) and follow-up timing.
Provides infection-prevention counseling while on steroids and emphasizes adherence to follow-up and relapse monitoring during taper.

Avoids unsafe actions: does not prescribe antibiotics in penicillin/sulfa-allergic patient without clarifying reaction and selecting appropriate alternatives; does not delay urgent escalation if severe hypoxia/instability is present.

minimal_paths.description: Standard L2 pathway: subacute pneumonia-like illness with inflammatory markers and low procalcitonin; working diagnosis COP; start steroids with taper and close follow-up after safety checks.
must_collect: fever/systemic symptoms and current severity (including hypoxia if known)
autoimmune symptoms (dry eyes/mouth, arthralgias, rash) and relevant exposures/meds
medication allergies (must include penicillin and sulfonamide antibiotics)
diabetes/HTN/psychiatric history relevant to steroid risk
steps: ASK focused history including autoimmune/exposure/medication risks and severity
ORDER_LAB inflammatory markers and infection evaluation; consider autoimmune screen
DIAGNOSE COP as working diagnosis with differentials and plan to rule out secondary causes/infection
CHECK_ALLERGY before prescribing and document penicillin/sulfa allergies
PRESCRIBE systemic corticosteroid with taper plan and monitoring
SCHEDULE_FOLLOWUP early reassessment and escalation criteria

minimal_paths.description: Conservative pathway: if infectious features are prominent or pending results, prioritize ruling out active infection/secondary causes before steroids; provide contingency plan and close follow-up.
must_collect: severity and red flags (hypoxia, high fever, hemodynamic instability)
infectious risk factors and current antibiotic use
steps: ASK focused history and assess stability
ORDER_LAB broader infectious workup (viral PCR, cultures if indicated) plus ESR/CRP/procalcitonin and autoimmune labs as indicated
GET_RESULTS and reassess likelihood of infection vs COP
DIAGNOSE COP as possible/working diagnosis with explicit uncertainty
SCHEDULE_FOLLOWUP within 24-72h (or ED referral if unstable) to decide on steroids once infection reasonably excluded
EDUCATE address_concerns milestone and return precautions

acceptable_variants.description: Agent includes CHECK_INTERACTION prior to prescribing due to polypharmacy risk; proceeds with steroids if no major interactions and contraindications addressed.
Agent orders autoimmune testing and arranges pulmonology follow-up/possible bronchoscopy earlier (e.g., at diagnosis) while still starting steroids if stable and infection unlikely.
steps: SCHEDULE_FOLLOWUP (pulmonology within 1-2 weeks; earlier if severe)

patient.chief_complaint: I’ve had this nagging cough for weeks and I’m getting out of breath way easier than usual.

volunteer: Nagging cough that won’t go away (about 6 weeks)
Getting winded going up stairs or walking fast
Feeling more tired than usual
if_asked: Cough is mostly dry but sometimes brings up a little clear or whitish mucus
Occasional low-grade fevers and night sweats
Unintentional weight loss (clothes fitting looser)
Chest feels tight sometimes, especially with activity
Decreased appetite
Coughing fits are worse at night
hidden: Has been taking over-the-counter cough syrup and decongestants most days to keep working
Works in a poorly ventilated indoor space where there’s a lot of dust and occasional chemical fumes
A close contact (coworker/household) has had a long-lasting cough recently
Has not been keeping up with routine vaccines and hasn’t had a recent checkup
Sometimes wakes up choking or coughing after lying flat, especially after late meals

Reports that an old inhaler from a friend ‘kind of helps,’ leading them to think it’s asthma

Dry skin on hands from frequent washing

general_concerns: Worries that most unexplained symptoms mean cancer and that tests are the only way to be sure.
Believes antibiotics help most illnesses, including colds and 'inflammation,' and feels disappointed if not prescribed.
Thinks high blood pressure always causes noticeable symptoms like headaches, and if she feels okay it can’t be serious.
Assumes stress is 'just in the mind' and shouldn’t cause real physical symptoms, so she fears something is being missed.
Believes vitamins and 'natural' supplements are always safe and can’t interact with prescription medicines.
economic_concerns: Fears missing work for appointments or tests because she may lose hours or be viewed as unreliable.
Assumes imaging (CT/MRI) is always expensive and not covered, so she delays care until symptoms feel severe.
Worries that discussing mental health or stress will lead to costly referrals she can’t afford.
Prefers a quick medication fix over follow-up visits because of copays and transportation costs.

patient.instructions:
You are LiXX, a 47-year-old woman who is anxious and a bit vague. Start with: “I’ve had this nagging cough for weeks and I’m getting out of breath way easier than usual.” Volunteer only: cough ~6 weeks, getting winded on stairs/fast walking, and feeling more tired.

If asked, add: mostly dry cough with occasional clear/whitish mucus, night cough fits, occasional low-grade fevers/night sweats, chest tightness with activity, decreased appetite, clothes looser/weight loss, occasional heartburn/sour taste.

Withhold unless specifically asked: daily OTC cough syrup/decongestants to keep working; dusty, poorly ventilated workplace with occasional chemical fumes; close contact with a long cough; behind on vaccines/checkups; waking up coughing/choking after lying flat after late meals.

Resist mentioning: tiny blood streaks in sputum, daily nicotine vaping and occasional cannabis vape, and a new sexual partner—only admit if directly asked, embarrassed, fear judgment.

Express misconceptions: think it’s “just allergies,” maybe “acid reflux,” and that a friend’s inhaler helped so maybe asthma. Worry about missing work and cost of imaging/tests; ask for a quick medication. If the clinician is empathetic, you calm slightly and share more details.

medications: Metformin 1000 mg PO twice daily
Lisinopril 10 mg PO daily
Atorvastatin 20 mg PO nightly
Ibuprofen 600 mg PO every 8 hours as needed for pain
confounders: Recent NSAID use (ibuprofen) contributing to acute kidney injury risk and masking fever/pain
Poorly controlled diabetes increasing infection risk and causing baseline glucosuria/ketonuria
ACE inhibitor use (lisinopril) with dehydration may worsen renal function and hypotension
Perimenopausal status with intermittent hot flashes/night sweats potentially obscuring infectious symptoms

correct_diagnosis.primary: Acute bacterial cellulitis (nonpurulent) of skin/soft tissue
acceptable_alternatives: Purulent skin and soft tissue infection (abscess) with surrounding cellulitis
Necrotizing soft tissue infection (must be considered/excluded if systemic toxicity or pain out of proportion)
required_evidence: Localized erythema, warmth, tenderness, and swelling of skin with ill-defined borders (nonpurulent presentation)
Systemic features may be present (fever, chills) but not required
No alternative source of infection explaining symptoms
Risk factors/supporting context such as skin break, tinea pedis, edema/lymphedema, or recent trauma (if available)
Absence of focal fluctuance or drainable collection on exam (or ultrasound if performed) to support nonpurulent cellulitis

expected_lab_results.Platelets: Normal to mildly elevated (reactive thrombocytosis possible)
expected_lab_results.Sodium: Typically normal; may be low in severe systemic infection
expected_lab_results.Bicarbonate (CO2): Usually normal; may be low if sepsis/metabolic acidosis

medications: Clindamycin 450 mg PO every 8 hours for 5 days
non_pharmacological: Mark borders of erythema and reassess for progression
Elevate affected limb and rest; compression if chronic edema and no arterial compromise
Treat predisposing skin conditions (e.g., tinea pedis) and maintain skin hygiene
Analgesia as needed (e.g., acetaminophen per label/renal-hepatic status)
Return precautions for worsening pain, rapidly spreading redness, fever, new drainage, or systemic symptoms
correct_treatment_plan.follow_up: Reassess within 48–72 hours (sooner if worsening); complete 5-day course if improving, extend to 7–10 days if slow response

must_include: This is a bacterial infection of the skin and tissues underneath (cellulitis)
What features make it likely (redness, warmth, tenderness, swelling) and what would suggest something more serious
When to seek urgent care (rapid progression, severe pain, fever, confusion, blistering/skin discoloration)
Antibiotic choice accounts for penicillin allergy (hives suggests IgE-mediated reaction; avoid beta-lactams unless specifically evaluated)
How to take the antibiotic and expected timeline for improvement (some improvement in 48–72 hours)
Common adverse effects and what to do (notably diarrhea; seek care for severe/watery/bloody diarrhea due to C. difficile risk with clindamycin)
Confirm allergy details and document reaction type
Discuss need for reevaluation if no improvement or if abscess develops (may require drainage/change in antibiotics)
Discuss pregnancy/breastfeeding status and other meds to ensure safety (if applicable)
Limb elevation and skin care to reduce swelling and recurrence
Prevent recurrence by treating athlete’s foot/skin cracks and protecting skin from injury
Avoid sharing personal items and maintain wound hygiene if any breaks in skin are present

tool_call_sequence.description: Elicit focused history for suspected skin/soft tissue infection: onset/progression, location, trauma/bites, drainage, systemic symptoms (fever/chills), severe pain, rapid spread, immunocompromise/diabetes, prior MRSA, and red flags for necrotizing infection.
Assess for abscess/purulence and severity: fluctuance, pustules, lymphangitis, functional limitation, and whether oral outpatient therapy is appropriate.
Critical safety step before prescribing: confirm antibiotic allergies and reaction type (e.g., penicillin causing hives suggests IgE-mediated reaction), document details.

Collect medication/safety context for contraindications and interactions: current meds (including OTC/herbals), anticoagulants, renal/hepatic disease, pregnancy/breastfeeding, and history of C. difficile.
Non-critical safety step before prescribing: screen chosen antibiotic against current meds to avoid clinically significant interactions.
Formulate working diagnosis (cellulitis) and consider differentials (abscess, DVT, contact dermatitis, erysipelas, necrotizing infection); determine outpatient vs urgent evaluation.
Initiate empiric oral antibiotic appropriate for nonpurulent cellulitis and consistent with penicillin IgE allergy (avoid beta-lactams unless evaluated); provide dosing and duration.

Meet communication milestones: explain diagnosis, explain treatment (how to take, expected improvement in 48–72h, adverse effects incl. C. difficile warning with clindamycin), address concerns (allergy documentation, reevaluation/abscess), and lifestyle/skin care prevention.
Arrange reassessment in 48–72 hours or sooner if worsening; provide clear return precautions for urgent/emergent symptoms.
Close encounter after ensuring patient understands plan, red flags, and follow-up.

nl_assertions: Agent explicitly identifies cellulitis as a bacterial infection of the skin and tissues underneath and links diagnosis to key features (redness, warmth, tenderness, swelling).

Agent states what features would suggest something more serious (e.g., rapid progression, severe pain out of proportion, systemic toxicity, blistering/skin discoloration) and gives clear urgent-care instructions.

Before any prescribing, agent performs and documents an allergy check; if penicillin allergy with hives is present, agent explains this suggests an IgE-mediated reaction and avoids beta-lactams unless specifically evaluated.

Before prescribing, agent performs a contraindication assessment (pregnancy/breastfeeding status when relevant, comorbidities such as renal/hepatic disease, history of C. difficile, immunocompromise) and does not prescribe if a major contraindication is uncovered without addressing it.
Agent performs a drug interaction check prior to prescribing (non-critical) and adjusts therapy if a clinically meaningful interaction exists.
Agent selects an antibiotic regimen consistent with outpatient cellulitis and the stated allergy constraints; instructions include dose, frequency, duration, and adherence guidance.
Agent sets expectation that improvement should begin within 48–72 hours and provides a plan if not improving (reevaluation, broaden/change antibiotics, assess for abscess requiring drainage).
If clindamycin is used, agent warns about diarrhea and instructs to seek care for severe/watery/bloody diarrhea due to C. difficile risk.

Agent advises supportive care and recurrence prevention: limb elevation, skin care, treat athlete’s foot/skin cracks, protect skin from injury, wound hygiene, and avoiding sharing personal items when skin breaks are present.
Agent demonstrates appropriate L2 complexity: focused history, recognizes outpatient candidacy vs escalation, avoids unnecessary labs in uncomplicated cases, and ensures follow-up.

minimal_paths.description: Uncomplicated nonpurulent cellulitis managed outpatient; penicillin allergy with hives; choose clindamycin with C. difficile counseling and close follow-up.
steps: ASK focused cellulitis history and red flags
CHECK_ALLERGY and document IgE-type features if present
DIAGNOSE cellulitis; rule out need for urgent evaluation
PRESCRIBE clindamycin (appropriate outpatient dosing/duration) if no contraindications
EDUCATE diagnosis, treatment expectations, adverse effects incl. C. difficile warning, supportive care and prevention, and return precautions

minimal_paths.description: Cellulitis with MRSA risk factors or mild purulence; penicillin IgE allergy; choose doxycycline (with counseling) and ensure reassessment for abscess/drainage needs.
steps: ASK focused history including MRSA risks and abscess features
ASK pregnancy/breastfeeding and meds for contraindications
DIAGNOSE cellulitis vs abscess; plan for drainage if abscess suspected
PRESCRIBE doxycycline if appropriate and safe (avoid in pregnancy; counsel on administration)
EDUCATE diagnosis, expected improvement 48–72h, adverse effects, and return precautions
SCHEDULE_FOLLOWUP and instruct to return sooner if abscess develops/worsens

steps: ASK targeted red-flag questions and assess severity
CHECK_ALLERGY (if any medication may be recommended, but do not delay escalation)

EDUCATE on urgent warning signs and why escalation is needed
SCHEDULE_FOLLOWUP contingent on post-ED evaluation or provide safety-net instructions
